guides.title: Getting Started with Organic Farming
guides.summary: A beginner's guide to transitioning your farm to certified organic practices.
steps: Assess your current soil health using a basic pH & nutrient test.
Remove synthetic fertilizers and pesticides from your supplies.
Introduce compost and green manure to improve soil fertility.
Plan crop rotation cycles to break pest and disease cycles.
Source certified organic seeds from reputable suppliers.
Keep detailed records for certification purposes.
tips: Start small with one field before converting entire farm.
Connect with local organic farming cooperatives for support.

guides.title: Drip Irrigation Setup for Small Farms
guides.summary: Step-by-step guide to installing a cost-effective drip irrigation system.
steps: Map your field and identify water source location.
Calculate water pressure and flow rate needed.
Purchase mainline pipes, emitters, and a filter kit.
Lay the mainline pipe along the field edges.
Connect lateral tubes to mainline at row intervals.
Place emitters near plant root zones.
Test the system and check for leaks before planting.
tips: Use a pressure regulator to avoid pipe bursts.
Flush the system weekly to prevent clogging.

guides.title: Seasonal Crop Planning for Maximum Yield
guides.summary: How to plan your crop calendar to maximize yield across all seasons.
steps: Review last season's results and soil analysis reports.
Identify high-demand crops in your local market.
Check rainfall patterns and expected temperature ranges.
Divide your land into rotation blocks.
Schedule planting dates 2 weeks before optimal period.
Prepare seed stock and fertilizer budget in advance.
tips: Keep a farming diary for year-on-year comparisons.
Attend local agricultural extension meetings for regional crop advice.

guides.title: Composting at Home & Farm Scale
guides.summary: Turn farm waste into rich compost to reduce costs and boost soil fertility.
steps: Collect brown materials: dry leaves, straw, cardboard.
Collect green materials: vegetable scraps, grass clippings.
Layer browns and greens in a 3:1 ratio by volume.
Keep the pile moist but not waterlogged.
Turn the pile every 2 weeks for aeration.
Compost is ready when it smells earthy and looks dark.
tips: Avoid meat and dairy in compost piles.
Chop materials into smaller pieces to speed up decomposition.

guides.title: Greenhouse Farming for Vegetables
guides.summary: Build and manage a simple tunnel greenhouse for year-round vegetable production.
steps: Select a sunny, level site sheltered from strong winds.
Choose between polythene tunnel or glass greenhouse.
Install raised beds or container systems inside.
Set up ventilation and temperature monitoring.
Plant high-value crops like tomatoes, peppers, and herbs.
Monitor humidity and water daily.
tips: Shade cloth reduces heat stress in summer.
Bees can be introduced for pollination.

guides.title: Water Conservation Techniques for Dry Regions
guides.summary: Practical water-saving strategies for farms in drought-prone areas.
steps: Conduct a water audit to identify wastage points.
Install rainwater harvesting tanks.
Apply mulch around crops to reduce evaporation.
Use deficit irrigation — water at 70% of crop needs.
Plant drought-resistant cover crops between rows.
Repair leaks and upgrade to drip or sprinkler systems.
tips: Water in early morning or evening to reduce evaporation.
Group crops with similar water needs in the same zone.

crops.name: Rice
crops.water: High – flooded paddies or heavy irrigation
crops.harvestTechnique: Cut stalks at base when grains turn golden; thresh by beating or machine.
crops.tips: Transplant seedlings after 3–4 weeks in nursery for better yield.

crops.name: Maize (Corn)
crops.varieties: Sweet Corn, Dent Corn, Flint Corn, Popcorn
crops.harvestTechnique: Hand-pick or machine harvest when husks are dry and kernels firm.
crops.tips: Plant in blocks rather than rows to ensure proper pollination.

crops.name: Tomato
crops.harvestTechnique: Pick when fully colored and slightly soft to touch.
crops.tips: Stake or cage plants early; remove suckers for larger fruits.

crops.name: Mango
crops.soil: Deep, well-drained loamy or sandy loam, pH 5.5–7.5
crops.growthDuration: 3–5 years to first fruit; harvests annually
crops.harvestTechnique: Hand-pick with a short stem when fruit gives slightly under pressure.
crops.tips: Avoid over-watering during flowering to prevent fruit drop.

crops.name: Sugarcane
crops.soil: Deep, well-drained loam to clay loam, pH 6.0–7.5
crops.harvestTechnique: Cut stalks at ground level with a sharp cane knife or harvester.
crops.tips: Ratoon cropping (re-sprouting) allows 2–3 harvests from one planting.

crops.name: Potato
crops.varieties: Russet, Yukon Gold, Red Pontiac, Fingerling
crops.harvestTechnique: Dig with a fork after vines die back; cure in a cool dark place for 2 weeks.
crops.tips: Hill soil around plants as they grow to prevent greening of tubers.

crops.name: Banana
crops.harvestTechnique: Cut the bunch when fingers are plump and round, while still green.
crops.tips: Remove the sucker (side shoot) leaving only 1–2 per plant for quality.

crops.name: Cotton
crops.varieties: Upland Cotton, Pima, Egyptian, Organic Cotton
crops.soil: Well-drained black or loamy soil, pH 5.8–8.0
crops.harvestTechnique: Hand-pick or machine harvest when bolls fully open and fibers fluffy.
crops.tips: Scout for bollworm regularly; apply IPM strategies early.

pests.name: Aphids
pests.symptoms: Curling or yellowing leaves, sticky honeydew residue, stunted growth.
pests.identification: Tiny (1–3mm), soft-bodied insects in green, black, or brown, found in clusters under leaves.
ipm: Encourage natural predators like ladybirds and lacewings.
Spray with neem oil solution (5ml per litre of water).
Use insecticidal soap spray as a first-line organic treatment.
Install yellow sticky traps to monitor populations.
Remove heavily infested plant parts promptly.
pests.organic: Neem oil, garlic spray, diatomaceous earth

pests.name: Fall Armyworm
pests.symptoms: Ragged holes in leaves, frass (excrement) in plant whorls, defoliation.
pests.identification: Caterpillar with an inverted Y-shape on head, brown/green striped, up to 4cm long.
ipm: Scout fields twice weekly, especially at crop emergence.
Apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) bio-pesticide when larvae are young.
Use pheromone traps to monitor adult moth populations.
Practice deep plowing to destroy pupae in soil.
Push-pull intercropping with Napier grass and Desmodium.
pests.organic: Bacillus thuringiensis, neem extract, ash in plant whorls

pests.name: Root-Knot Nematodes
pests.symptoms: Wilting despite adequate water, stunted growth, root galls (knots) on roots.
pests.identification: Microscopic worms; confirm by pulling plants and inspecting roots for round galls.
ipm: Rotate crops with non-host plants like maize or cereals.
Incorporate marigold plants as a bio-fumigant cover crop.
Solarize soil with clear plastic for 6 weeks before planting.
Use resistant vegetable varieties where available.
Apply Trichoderma-based bio-fungicide to soil at planting.
pests.organic: Marigold green manure, neem cake, Trichoderma harzianum

pests.name: Powdery Mildew
pests.symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves and stems; leaves yellow then die.
pests.identification: Distinct white talc-like powder on upper leaf surface, usually starting in patches.
ipm: Plant resistant varieties when available.
Ensure good air circulation with proper plant spacing.
Apply baking soda spray (1 tsp per litre of water) at first signs.
Avoid overhead watering; water at base of plants.
Remove and destroy infected plant material.
pests.organic: Baking soda spray, potassium bicarbonate, sulfur dust

pests.name: Locust Swarms
pests.symptoms: Rapid total defoliation of crops, darkening skies when swarms approach.
pests.identification: Large grasshopper-like insects (4–8cm), yellow or green, travelling in massive groups.
ipm: Monitor national locust forecasting bulletins and early warnings.
Coordinate with government authorities for aerial spraying programs.
Use noise and fire at field perimeters to deter swarms.
Protect seedlings with netting during invasion threats.
Apply Metarhizium (bio-insecticide fungus) when swarms land.

pests.name: Tomato Late Blight
pests.symptoms: Water-soaked dark lesions on leaves and stems; white mold on undersides; rapid plant collapse.
pests.identification: Brown-black patches on leaves with yellow borders; white fuzzy growth visible under humid conditions.
ipm: Use certified blight-free seed or resistant varieties.
Avoid overhead irrigation; drip irrigate instead.
Apply copper-based fungicide as a preventive spray.
Space plants widely for good air flow.
Destroy all infected plant material immediately.
